attributes.stem:
A 78-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with an eight-day history of constipation and two-day history of colicky abdominal pain. She is a nursing home resident with limited mobility. She normally takes coloxyl/senna daily for constipation but this has been ineffective recently. She has previously found enemas too uncomfortable to tolerate. Her medical history includes hypertension and arthritis. Her medications include perindopril and meloxicam.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 136/90 mmHg, and temperature 37.2°C. Her abdomen is soft with no palpable masses. Digital rectal examination reveals a rectum loaded with firm stools. A plain abdominal X-ray demonstrates faecal loading throughout the colon.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Increased dosage of coloxyl and senna
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with faecal impaction, a common problem in older, less mobile individuals. The most appropriate next step in management is polyethylene glycol bowel preparation. This is a gentle and effective method for managing faecal impaction, especially when it is extensive as shown on this patient's X-ray. Polyethylene glycol works through an osmotic effect, drawing water into the intestinal lumen to soften and loosen the impacted faecal material.

Sigmoidoscopy is not appropriate as the immediate priority is to relieve the faecal impaction before considering any further investigations. While sigmoidoscopy might be considered later to rule out underlying pathology, it is not the next step in acute management.

Manual faecal disimpaction can be effective for distal impaction, but is not suitable in this case where there is proximal faecal loading throughout the colon. It would be painful and ineffective for extensive impaction, potentially requiring general anaesthesia.

Increased dosage of coloxyl and senna is unlikely to be effective given that the patient's regular laxatives have already failed to prevent this impaction. A stronger intervention is needed at this point.

Sodium phosphate enema is not recommended here as the patient has previously found enemas too uncomfortable to tolerate. Moreover, an enema would only address distal impaction and not the proximal loading seen on X-ray.

After relieving the impaction with polyethylene glycol, it would be important to address the underlying causes of chronic constipation in this patient. This might include reviewing her medications (particularly meloxicam, which can cause constipation), increasing fluid intake, improving dietary fibre, encouraging mobility where possible, and considering a regular bowel regimen to prevent recurrence.
learningPoints: Standard bowel preparation with polyethylene glycol is a simple, effective management for faecal impaction in older patients.
Manual faecal disimpaction is only appropriate for distal impaction, not for proximal faecal loading throughout the colon.
references: Brandt LJ, Prather CM, Quigley EM, et al. Systematic review on the management of chronic constipation in North America. Am J Gastroenterol 2005; 100 Suppl 1:S5. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16008641/

Rao SS, Rattanakovit K, Patcharatrakul T. Diagnosis and management of chronic constipation in adults. Nat Rev Gastroenterol Hepatol 2016; 13:295. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27033126/
Setya A, Mathew G, Cagir B. Fecal Impaction.  In: StatPearls [Online]. [Updated 2023 Jul 4]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448094/
